title: Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model

learningObjectives.text: Compare synchronous versus asynchronous work using a new example and justify the result.
Apply ajax and apis using a new example and justify the result.
Trace and explain http requests and responses using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in asynchronous javascript and the web request model recur throughout later JavaScript work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **Asynchronous JavaScript** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Synchronous versus asynchronous work** → **AJAX and APIs** → **HTTP requests and responses**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Synchronous versus asynchronous work
sections.body: Synchronous code completes on the current stack. Asynchronous operations begin work that finishes later, allowing the runtime to continue.

sections.title: AJAX and APIs
sections.body: AJAX is the practice of exchanging data without replacing the whole page. An API defines endpoints, inputs, outputs, status codes, and error behavior.

sections.title: HTTP requests and responses
sections.body: Requests contain a method, URL, headers, and optional body; responses contain status, headers, and body. Network success and application success differ.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The function separates HTTP status validation from body parsing.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model connects Synchronous versus asynchronous work, AJAX and APIs, HTTP requests and responses.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Synchronous versus asynchronous work” in the context of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model?

choices.label: Synchronous code completes on the current stack. Asynchronous operations begin work that finishes later, allowing the runtime to continue.
choices.feedback: Synchronous versus asynchronous work is best understood as follows: Synchronous code completes on the current stack. Asynchronous operations begin work that finishes later, allowing the runtime to continue.

choices.label: Functional principles favor pure transformations, immutability, explicit inputs, and composition where they improve predictability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Synchronous versus asynchronous work.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Synchronous versus asynchronous work.

choices.label: The DOM is a tree of node objects connected through parent, child, and sibling relationships. Traversal APIs expose those relationships.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Synchronous versus asynchronous work.

questions.explanation: Synchronous versus asynchronous work is best understood as follows: Synchronous code completes on the current stack. Asynchronous operations begin work that finishes later, allowing the runtime to continue.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

choices.label: Apply Synchronous versus asynchronous work deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model?

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Synchronous versus asynchronous work, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model?

choices.label: Use Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

choices.label: Avoid Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model.

choices.label: Use Asynchronous JavaScript and the Web Request Model when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
